input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted as a tele‑operated platform with advanced sensor and control technology. Its hardware hierarchy comprises a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and dedicated autonomy elements (sensors, actuators, and ECU). The vehicle is used to demonstrate Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) functions, providing real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and proximity object tracks, which makes it appropriate for digital‑twin replication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) that displays real‑time vehicle state data, projected path, and detected object tracks, and enables users to view, save, and generate reports of historic data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation based on inter‑vehicle distance) runs on the DT (e.g., cloud resources) and returns the computed speed to the physical vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI allows operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency‑brake commands, requiring low‑latency bidirectional communication. Service users are classified as operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting via data collection and monitoring).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation requires low‑latency end‑to‑end communication between vehicle and twin, robust data‑collection and reporting infrastructure, and safety‑critical design considerations. The system leverages cloud computing resources for off‑loaded ACC processing and a graphical UI for visualization and command issuance. No specific software platforms or protocols are enumerated in the provided document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin requires that all devices share a common notion of time with clock synchronization accuracy of ±100 ms; this accuracy limit is imposed by the hardware and enables computation offloading among devices. Although many safety‑critical vehicular standards demand tighter synchronization, the system itself operates with the 100 ms bound, and no additional latency budgets for other services are specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based model with three top‑level entities—User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and Physical Twin Entity—each instantiated on separate hardware nodes. Within each entity multiple Functional Entities (e.g., User Interface FE split into visualization and control‑panel sub‑FE) are realized as ROS2 packages/nodes, yielding a decentralized, multi‑instance architecture.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Overall orchestration is based on the ISO 23247 reference architecture, realized through ROS2. Each Functional Entity is packaged as an individual ROS2 package containing one or more ROS2 nodes; the three entities are deployed on distinct compute platforms, enabling resource‑specific management (sensors/actuators in the Physical Twin Entity, data management in the Digital Twin Entity, UI in the User Entity). Coordination among entities is achieved via ROS2 middleware message passing.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Inter‑entity communication uses ROS2, while integration with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins can be performed through standard interfaces such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as suggested by ISO 23247; no further protocol specifics are detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates a geometric point‑cloud model generated from the Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR and the Intel Deepsense D435i depth camera. Raw point clouds are processed by a Euclidean clustering algorithm to segment individual objects; each cluster is then tracked over time with a linear Kalman filter that predicts object state (position, velocity) and updates it with new measurements. The resulting object tracks together with the ego‑vehicle state (pose, speed) are persisted in a relational SQL database that serves as the central data store for the twin. A 3‑D visualization component consumes the stored tracks and renders them in a virtual scene to provide operators with a real‑time spatial view of the environment. The model hierarchy links the low‑level geometric data to higher‑level behavioral representations (e.g., motion trajectories) used for decision‑making.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The ISO 23247 reference architecture defines a Data Collection and Device Control Entity whose Functional Entities are responsible for actuating the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME). The standard does not prescribe specific actuator types, control ranges, or safety constraints, but mentions that communication protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture can be used to implement remote control of the OME.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing layer consists of a Hokuyo UST‑10LX 2‑D LiDAR (range up to 10 m, angular resolution 0.25°, 40 Hz scan rate) and an Intel RealSense D435i depth camera (RGB‑D, depth range 0.2‑10 m, 30 fps). Both sensors are mounted on the autonomous vehicle platform to provide a full 360° perception envelope. LiDAR returns 2‑D range scans that are converted to 3‑D point clouds using known mounting geometry; the depth camera supplies dense 3‑D depth images. Sensor data are streamed over Ethernet/USB to the Data Collection Entity where they are timestamped, synchronized, and forwarded to the processing pipeline at their native frequencies.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data (LiDAR point‑cloud, motor status, steering servo position, vehicle state) generated by the Physical Twin Entity is transmitted to the Digital Twin Entity over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. Within the DT the data streams are received by the Data Translation functional entity, which serializes the messages, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. The raw LiDAR point cloud is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (Data Pre‑Processing FE) before being supplied to higher‑level services such as Data Monitoring. Communication between software components inside the DT uses a ROS 2 middleware network. No explicit transmission frequency or event‑trigger description is provided.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Digital Twin (e.g., ACC enable/disable, desired speed set‑point, emergency braking) are sent back to the Physical Twin over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link. The commands are formatted by the Data Translation component to match the vehicle’s control message schema and are consumed by the Actuation functional entity (Actuation FE) which drives the motor and steering servos. The DT UI (Control Panel) provides the operator interface for issuing these commands. Details on validation, authentication, conflict resolution, or acknowledgement of command execution are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT’s internal software components communicate via a ROS 2 network, while inter‑entity communication between the Physical Twin and the Digital Twin uses a Wi‑Fi network employing UDP at the transport layer. The architecture is illustrated in Figure 2, showing a layered deployment where sensor data flows from the physical sensors to the DT services. No quantitative bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocol specifications are given.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The system demonstrates computation off‑loading of the ACC functionality to a Digital Twin that can run on better‑suited equipment such as cloud resources, implying a cloud‑based hosting model. The documents mention the need for low end‑to‑end latency but do not provide concrete details on the hosting platform, computational or storage sizing, scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin Entity is accessed by a User Entity that provides interfaces for users to monitor data and control the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME). Functional Entities within the DT perform data transformation to present information in a human‑interpretable format, enabling users to observe operational status and issue actuation commands. Decision support is thus realized through direct monitoring dashboards and control interfaces, without explicit mention of advanced analytics or confidence metrics in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system follows the ISO 23247 standard (Digital Twin framework for manufacturing), specifically leveraging Part 2 (Reference Architecture) which defines an entity‑based architecture comprising Observable Manufacturing Elements, Data Collection and Device Control Entities, Digital Twin Entities, and User Entities, each built from Functional Entities. The standard permits the use of technologies such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for data exchange, indicating adherence to open industrial communication standards.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin System shall be built respecting the safety-critical nature of the vehicle function, complying with ISO 15622:2018 “Intelligent Transport Systems – Adaptive Cruise Control Systems – Performance Requirements and Test Procedures,” which defines the performance and test criteria that the twin must meet for ACC functionality. In addition, ISO 26262‑1:2018 “Road vehicles – Functional safety” governs the safety lifecycle, requiring hazard analysis, risk assessment, and the implementation of fail-safe mechanisms for safety-critical components of the twin. These standards together mandate that the twin’s behavior be validated against ACC safety requirements and that functional‑safety processes be applied, although specific cybersecurity protocols or authentication mechanisms are not detailed in the cited documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

It correctly identifies the autonomy components and the system's purpose for demonstrating ACC with specific data outputs. The description is highly specific and detailed based on the text. Quotes: '1/10-scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis', 'autonomy components include an NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna... and NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer'.

The extraction comprehensively covers the three core services defined in the text: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. It correctly details the capabilities of the UI, the offloading of the ACC algorithm (lead vehicle detection, speed computation), and the specific remote commands (emergency brake). It also accurately classifies users into operators and developers. Quotes: 'Data Monitoring: Monitoring ACC-relevant data... UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'Computation Offloading... ACC system determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed', 'users of our system can be grouped into two classes: operators and developers'.

The extraction correctly mentions high-level requirements like low latency and cloud computing, but it contains a critical error in the final sentence: 'No specific software platforms or protocols are enumerated in the provided document.' The text explicitly lists several tools, including 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)', 'Python 3.8', 'C++', and 'MATLAB/Simulink'. Claiming these are missing makes the extraction factually incorrect regarding the provided sources.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

It correctly identifies the 100 ms accuracy limit, the hardware limitation as the cause, and the context of computation offloading. Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms' and 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The description is highly specific and accurate, detailing the three entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin), their deployment on separate hardware, and the implementation of Functional Entities as ROS2 packages. It correctly notes the decomposition of the User Interface FE into visualization and control-panel subcomponents.

The extraction provides an in-depth technical explanation of the system orchestration, accurately linking the ISO 23247 RA to the ROS2 implementation. It correctly describes the mapping of responsibilities to entities, the distinct compute platforms for deployment, and the ROS2 middleware message passing for coordination.

The extraction correctly identifies ROS2 as the primary protocol for communication. It accurately cites the ISO 23247 suggestion of OPC UA for interoperability (specifically mentioning the Data Translation FE context), though it generalizes this to external enterprise integration. The statement that no further protocol specifics are detailed is supported by the text.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE.
